source: EIA — World Oil Transit Chokepoints (volumes are pre-crisis reference values, 2023–H1 2025)
notes: The site's thesis as a table: every major oil chokepoint has an escape — another sea lane, a parallel pipeline, or both — except Hormuz. The validator enforces that exactly one chokepoint has no sea alternative, and that it is Hormuz.

chokepoints.name: Strait of Hormuz
chokepoints.bypass_note: Petroline + ADCOP spare (the whole escape valve)
chokepoints.closure_record: Never fully closed through 45 years of threats — until 2026. The first actual closure is the current crisis.

chokepoints.name: Strait of Malacca
chokepoints.closure_record: Never closed; piracy-era harassment at worst. Rerouting costs days, not barrels.

chokepoints.name: Suez Canal + SUMED
chokepoints.closure_record: Closed 1956–57 and 1967–1975 (eight years); Ever Given 2021 (six days). The world rerouted and lived.

chokepoints.name: Bab el-Mandeb
chokepoints.closure_record: 2023–25 Houthi campaign roughly halved transits — traffic diverted, cargoes still arrived.

chokepoints.name: Panama Canal
chokepoints.closure_record: 2023–24 drought cut transits ~30% — products rerouted at a freight cost.
